1.question: After reading the group’s procedure, describe what additional information you would need in order to replicate the experiment. Make sure to include at least three pieces of information.

human_rubrics.3: The response describes three additional pieces of information that would be needed to accurately replicate the experiment.
human_rubrics.2: The response describes two additional pieces of information that would be needed to accurately replicate the experiment.
human_rubrics.1: The response describes one additional piece of information that would be needed to accurately replicate the experiment.
human_rubrics.0: The response describes little or no accurate or relevant information from the acid rain investigation.

rubrics.0: Responses give no specific procedural details needed to replicate the acid-rain experiment and often include unrelated statements (for example asking for a hypothesis or saying “I don't know”). They fail to identify any of the key missing items from the reference answers such as type or amount of vinegar, sample identity or size, exposure time, container dimensions, rinsing procedure, or measurement methods.
rubrics.1: Responses identify exactly one specific procedural detail that is missing for replication — for example naming the type of vinegar OR the exact volume of vinegar OR the duration the samples are submerged. They clearly state that single item but omit other essential parameters like sample size/shape, container specifications, rinsing steps, or measurement procedures that are also needed to reproduce the experiment.
rubrics.2: Responses identify two distinct, specific pieces of missing procedural information — for example specifying both what the samples are and how much vinegar to use, or the exposure time and the instrument for measuring mass. They demonstrate partial sufficiency for replication but still omit at least one other critical element (such as container dimensions, vinegar concentration, rinsing instructions, or environmental conditions) required for full reproducibility.
rubrics.3: Responses list three specific procedural details that would enable replication, such as the material and size of the samples, the exact volume or concentration of vinegar to use, and the duration or environmental conditions (including container type or temperature) for the exposure. They differ from lower scores by covering multiple different categories of missing information (sample identity/size, vinegar details, and timing/storage/measurement instructions) rather than repeating similar items.

changes.0: Shifted wording to response-perspective; specified examples of irrelevant content and listed concrete missing parameters (vinegar type/amount, sample identity, time, containers, rinsing, measurements).
changes.1: Converted to response-perspective and made it explicit that only one specific procedural detail is identified; added examples (vinegar type/volume/duration) and noted omission of other required parameters.
changes.2: Rewrote from response-perspective to state two distinct pieces are present; added examples of paired items and clarified what kinds of critical elements may still be missing for full replication.
changes.3: Reframed from response-perspective to require three specific procedural details; gave concrete examples (sample size, vinegar volume/concentration, duration/environment) and emphasized coverage across different categories of information.

2.question: a. Draw a conclusion based on the student’s data. b. Describe two ways the student could have improved the experimental design and/or validity of the results.

human_rubrics.3: The response draws a valid conclusion supported by the student’s data and describes two ways the student could have improved the experimental design and/or the validity of the results.
human_rubrics.2: The response draws a valid conclusion supported by the student’s data and describes one way the student could have improved the experimental design and/or the validity of the results. -or- The response describes two ways the student could have improved the experimental design and/or the validity of the results but fails to draw or incorrectly draws a conclusion from the student’s data.
human_rubrics.1: The response draws a valid conclusion supported by the student’s data but fails to describe, or incorrectly describes, how the student could have improved the experimental design and/or the validity of the results. -or- The response describes one way the student could have improved the experimental design and/or the validity of the results but fails to draw or incorrectly draws a conclusion from the student’s data.
human_rubrics.0: The response provides little or no correct information from the polymer investigation.

rubrics.0: Responses give little or no correct information about the experiment’s results or the relationship between material type and mass change/stretching; they fail to identify which material reacted more or less and do not propose plausible improvements to experimental design or validity. Responses may contain irrelevant statements or misunderstand the data so that neither a supported conclusion nor realistic suggestions (e.g., standardize sample size, increase trials) are provided.
rubrics.1: Responses either state a reasonable data-based conclusion (for example, naming which plastic stretched most or which material lost more mass) but provide no or incorrect suggestions for improving design, or they offer one vague or inappropriate improvement while failing to draw a correct, data-supported conclusion. Responses at this level show partial understanding of the data or of experimental controls but miss one of the two required parts or give an improvement that would not increase validity (e.g., ‘use a better brand’ without specifics).
rubrics.2: Responses correctly identify a conclusion supported by the student’s data (such as which material reacted most/least to vinegar or which plastic stretched most) and provide one clear, relevant way to improve the design (for example, standardize sample size/length, report weight used, or increase number of trials), or they list two plausible improvements but fail to make a correct, data-supported conclusion. Responses here demonstrate reasonable interpretation of the results or reasonable design fixes but are missing either a second valid improvement or a correct conclusion.
rubrics.3: Responses draw a valid conclusion directly supported by the student’s data (for example, naming the relative stretching or mass-change of each material) and describe two specific, relevant improvements that would increase experimental validity, such as standardizing sample size/length, specifying weight and exposure time, increasing the number of trials, or controlling environmental conditions and vinegar concentration. Responses clearly link the suggested improvements to how they would strengthen the reliability or accuracy of the observed results.

changes.0: Changed teacher-perspective to response-perspective; added specificity about failing to identify which material reacted and lacking plausible improvements; clarified examples of missing content.
changes.1: Shifted wording to responses-perspective; specified the two possible partial-success patterns (correct conclusion but no valid improvements, or one vague improvement but no correct conclusion) and gave concrete examples of weak improvements.
changes.2: Reworded to focus on responses, specified acceptable content (correct conclusion + one clear improvement OR two improvements without conclusion) and gave concrete examples of valid improvements to distinguish from adjacent levels.
changes.3: Made response-perspective explicit; specified that both a data-supported conclusion and two specific, relevant improvements are required and listed example improvements tied to experimental validity.

3.question: Explain how pandas in China are similar to koalas in Australia and how they both are different from pythons. Support your response with information from the article.

human_rubrics.2: The response demonstrates an exploration or development of the ideas presented in the text; a strong conceptual understanding by the inclusion of specific relevant information from the text; an extension of ideas that may include extensive and/or insightful inferences, connections between ideas in the text, and references to prior knowledge and/or experiences.
human_rubrics.1: The response demonstrates some exploration or development of ideas presented in the text; a fundamental understanding by the inclusion of some relevant information from the text; an extension of ideas that lacks depth, although may include some inferences, connections between ideas in the text, or references to prior knowledge and/or experiences.
human_rubrics.0: The response demonstrates limited or no exploration or development of ideas presented in the text; limited or no understanding of the text, may be illogical, vague, or irrelevant; possible incomplete or limited inferences, connections between ideas in the text, or references to prior knowledge and/or experiences.

rubrics.0: Responses show little or no accurate use of the article’s information about diet, habitat, or taxonomy; they may only offer vague statements (for example, just saying “specialist”) or irrelevant comments without explaining that pandas eat bamboo and koalas eat eucalyptus. These responses often confuse or omit how pythons differ (reptile, carnivore, hunter) and provide no concrete examples or textual evidence from the article.
rubrics.1: Responses include some correct article-based points such as pandas eating bamboo and koalas eating eucalyptus and that pythons differ (for example, being carnivorous or more adaptable), but they present these facts without fully explaining the shared mammalian traits (arboreal/forested habitat, specialist feeding) or the specific contrasts with pythons. They may repeat basic information or contain minor inaccuracies and typically lack explicit connections or supporting examples from the article about reproduction, conservation, or feeding strategies.
rubrics.2: Responses accurately use specific information from the article to explain similarities (both pandas and koalas are herbivorous, specialist mammal feeders tied to forest/arboreal habitats — pandas on bamboo, koalas on eucalyptus) and clear contrasts with pythons (reptilian, carnivorous, generalist diet, hunting by constriction, greater adaptability). They draw appropriate inferences or make explicit connections to conservation status, reproductive constraints, or habitat dependence, and cite concrete examples from the text to support those distinctions.

changes.0: Converted teacher-focused language to response-focused wording; specified missing elements (diet, habitat, taxonomy) and noted typical vague or incorrect phrasing seen in examples.
changes.1: Shifted to response-perspective and added specificity about which correct facts appear and what is incomplete (lack of links to mammal traits, reproduction, conservation), noting tendency for minor errors or unsupported claims.
changes.2: Rewrote from the responses' viewpoint and made the expectations concrete by naming the article facts to include (bamboo, eucalyptus, arboreal habitat, constriction, adaptability) and requiring explicit connections/inferences supported by examples.

4.question: Explain the significance of the word “invasive” to the rest of the article. Support your response with information from the article.

human_rubrics.2: The response demonstrates: a thoughtful and thorough examination of the text, uses information to move beyond the boundaries of the text, possible consideration of how, why or for whom the text was written, an ability to evaluate explicit and implicit information and/or makes insightful judgments about the text, possible evaluation of the author’s craft, including literary devices and/or a projection of the implications raised.
human_rubrics.1: The response demonstrates: sufficient, though limited, examination of the text, some attempt to use information to move beyond the boundaries of the text, possible consideration of how, why, or for whom the text was written, some ability to evaluate explicit and implicit information and/or makes plausible judgments about the text, possible evaluation of the author’s craft, including literary devices and/or a projection of the implications raised.
human_rubrics.0: The response demonstrates: a cursory examination of the text, does not move beyond the boundaries of the text, little or no consideration of how, why, or for whom the text was written, little or no ability to evaluate explicit and implicit information and/or makes superficial judgments about the text, the author’s craft, including literary devices and/or a projection of the implications raised, possible simplistic, unsupported, illogical, vague, and/or irrelevant information.

rubrics.0: Responses offer vague, incorrect, or purely personal statements about the word "invasive," usually only restating the term or giving unrelated opinions and failing to connect the word to the article’s points about non‑native pythons, ecosystem harm, or biodiversity loss.
rubrics.1: Responses identify that "invasive" refers to species entering new habitats and sometimes note examples like pythons or displacement of native animals, but they give only limited explanation or weak textual support and do not fully explain the article’s claims about ecological disruption or management implications.
rubrics.2: Responses clearly explain that "invasive" signals ecological harm from non‑native species, using article evidence (for example, pythons in the Everglades, threats to endangered species, and the surrounding controversy) and synthesize how the term frames concerns about biodiversity loss, ecosystem change, and the need for management or policy responses.

changes.0: Changed to response‑perspective; specified common errors (vague restatement, personal opinion) and added explicit reference to missing connections with pythons, ecosystem harm, and biodiversity.
changes.1: Changed to response‑perspective; clarified that responses show partial understanding (identify invasion and examples) but lack explanation or textual support, distinguishing from both misunderstanding and full synthesis.
changes.2: Changed to response‑perspective; made rubric explicitly reference article evidence (pythons, endangered species, controversy) and emphasized synthesis of ecological and management implications to separate it from limited responses.

5.question: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.

rubrics.0: Responses present information that is irrelevant or incorrect to protein synthesis beginning with mRNA leaving the nucleus, for example describing ATP production, mitochondria-only processes, or garbled organelle functions. These responses fail to identify any of the core steps from the reference (mRNA export, ribosome binding and codon reading, tRNA anticodon pairing with codons, peptide-bond formation/stop codon), so they cannot be mapped to the required sequence of events.
rubrics.1: Responses mention one or two elements from the correct pathway (for example that mRNA leaves the nucleus or that mRNA goes to ribosomes/tRNA finds complementary bases) but omit or misstate other major steps. They often give an incomplete order or incorrect mechanism (e.g., mRNA is said to be copied at the ribosome, or ER/Golgi are incorrectly emphasized) and lack explicit description of codon reading, amino-acid delivery, peptide-bond formation, or reaching a stop codon.
rubrics.2: Responses accurately describe three of the four major steps: typically that mRNA goes to ribosomes and is read as codons, that tRNA brings matching amino acids via anticodon pairing, and that amino acids become joined; however they omit or under-explain one key element (commonly the initial exit from the nucleus, the explicit codon/anticodon pairing detail, or explicit mention of a stop codon halting translation). The sequence and basic mechanisms are mostly correct but missing one distinct required step or a clear statement of termination.
rubrics.3: Responses clearly and correctly state all four major steps: mRNA exits the nucleus and serves as a cytoplasmic template, ribosomes bind and read mRNA codons, tRNAs bring amino acids matching codons via anticodons, and the ribosome forms peptide bonds to elongate the polypeptide until a stop codon ends translation. Explanations include the order of events and the key mechanisms (codon reading, anticodon pairing, peptide-bond formation and termination) with no major omissions.

changes.0: Shifted to response-perspective; specified examples of irrelevant errors (ATP, mitochondria); clarified absence of any of the four reference steps to distinguish from higher scores.
changes.1: Changed to response-perspective; specified which one or two correct elements appear and common incorrect claims (mRNA copied at ribosome, ER/Golgi focus); stressed missing mechanism/order to separate from score 0 and 2.
changes.2: Changed to response-perspective; specified which three steps are typically present and which single step is commonly missing (nuclear exit, codon detail, or stop codon); clarified that sequence is mostly correct but incomplete.
changes.3: Changed to response-perspective; made explicit all four required steps and included key mechanisms (codon reading, anticodon pairing, peptide-bond formation, termination) to distinguish from the omission in the previous level.

6.question: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.

rubrics.0: Responses fail to identify any valid membrane-transport processes from the reference answers or provide only unrelated or incorrect biological concepts (e.g., mitosis, meiosis, or misassigned organelle functions), so no recognizable mechanism for moving substances across the cell membrane is presented.
rubrics.1: Responses identify or attempt to describe one legitimate mechanism (for example, osmosis, diffusion, active transport, facilitated diffusion, or endo-/exocytosis) but give incomplete, inaccurate, or very brief explanations that omit key distinguishing details such as energy use, direction relative to a concentration gradient, or involvement of transport proteins.
rubrics.2: Responses correctly name and describe two distinct membrane-transport processes and include at least one accurate detail for each (for example that osmosis moves water or that active transport requires energy), but the third process is missing, incorrect, or described with significant vagueness or error.
rubrics.3: Responses clearly name and describe three distinct processes used to move substances across the cell membrane and include key, accurate details such as whether the process requires energy (active vs. passive), whether transport proteins are involved (facilitated diffusion, channels/carriers), or that osmosis specifically moves water.

changes.0: Flipped teacher-perspective to response-perspective; specified examples of irrelevant answers and clarified that no valid transport mechanism is present.
changes.1: Changed wording to response-perspective; added concrete examples of acceptable single processes and emphasized missing distinguishing details (energy, gradient, transport proteins).
changes.2: Rewrote from response-perspective; specified that two processes must be correctly named with at least one accurate detail and clarified that the third is missing or incorrect to distinguish from full credit.
changes.3: Converted to response-perspective and added concrete criteria (three distinct processes with accurate key details like energy use, transport proteins, or osmosis-specific water movement) to differentiate from the two-process level.

7.question: Identify ONE trait that can describe Rose based on her conversations with Anna or Aunt Kolab. Include ONE detail from the story that supports your answer.

human_rubrics.2: Identifies one character trait of Rose and includes story details that adequately support the answer, related to the conversations with Anna or Aunt Kolab.
human_rubrics.1: Identifies one character trait of Rose.
human_rubrics.0: No character trait of Rose is identified.

rubrics.0: Responses do not identify a clear character trait of Rose; they offer summaries, unrelated statements, or descriptions of events without naming a trait or tying it to Rose’s conversations with Anna or Aunt Kolab.
rubrics.1: Responses name one trait for Rose (e.g., responsible, stressed, thoughtful) but give little or no specific evidence from her conversations with Anna or Aunt Kolab, or the supporting detail is vague or only loosely connected to the dialogue.
rubrics.2: Responses name one appropriate trait for Rose and cite a specific supporting detail from the story—such as a quoted line or a described moment in her talk with Anna or Aunt Kolab—that clearly illustrates the named trait (for example, Rose staying silent to avoid hurting her aunt shows thoughtfulness).

changes.0: Switched to response-perspective; clarified that answers either omit a trait or fail to connect claims to Rose’s conversations; added specificity about the absence of conversation-based evidence.
changes.1: Switched to response-perspective; specified that a single trait is named but supporting evidence from Anna/Aunt Kolab conversations is missing or vague; distinguished from 0 by presence of a named trait and from 2 by weak evidence.
changes.2: Switched to response-perspective; required one trait plus a concrete story detail tied to Rose’s conversations with Anna or Aunt Kolab; gave an explicit example to show the expected quality of evidence and distinguished from 1 by strength of support.

8.question: During the story, the reader gets background information about Mr. Leonard. Explain the effect that background information has on Paul. Support your response with details from the story.

human_rubrics.2: Proficient: The response fulfills all the requirements of the task. The information given is text-based and relevant to the task.
human_rubrics.1: Partially Proficient: The response fulfills some of the requirements of the task, but some of the information may be too general, too simplistic, or not supported by the text.
human_rubrics.0: Not Proficient: The response does not fulfill the requirements of the task because it contains information that is inaccurate, incomplete, and/or missing altogether.

rubrics.0: Responses do not convincingly connect Mr. Leonard's background to Paul's feelings or actions, often contain factual errors about Mr. Leonard or the plot, or offer only irrelevant or missing evidence from the story.
rubrics.1: Responses show a basic recognition that Mr. Leonard's past affects Paul (for example, that Paul feels sympathy or motivation) but remain vague or general and fail to support the claim with specific events, quotations, or clear explanation of how the background led Paul to act.
rubrics.2: Responses clearly explain how learning about Mr. Leonard's illiteracy, athletic history, or sacrifices causes Paul to empathize, admire, or decide to help/train with him, and they support these inferences with text-based details or specific moments from the story.

changes.0: Shifted wording to response-perspective; specified that errors, irrelevance, or omission of textual evidence characterize these responses.
changes.1: Changed to response-perspective; clarified that responses show a general connection but lack specific story-based support or explanation distinguishing them from proficient answers.
changes.2: Rewrote from response-perspective; added concrete content references (illiteracy, athletic past, sacrifices) and emphasized requirement for text-based support to distinguish from partial responses.

9.question: How does the author organize the article? Support your response with details from the article.

human_rubrics.2: Proficient: The response fulfills all the requirements of the task. The information given is text-based and relevant to the task.
human_rubrics.1: Partially Proficient: The response fulfills some of the requirements of the task, but some of the information may be too general, too simplistic, or not supported by the text.
human_rubrics.0: Not Proficient: The response does not fulfill the requirements of the task because it contains information that is inaccurate, incomplete, and/or missing altogether.

rubrics.0: Responses do not accurately describe how the article is organized, offering unrelated, confused, or incomplete statements (for example, vague mentions of dates or general praise rather than a description of introduction, sections, headings, or sequence) and provide no clear text-based support for any organizational claim.
rubrics.1: Responses identify a plausible organizational pattern (such as an introduction followed by background and separate sections or the use of headings) but remain general or simplistic, often lacking specific section titles, textual examples, or clear explanation of the sequence that would show how the parts develop the main idea.
rubrics.2: Responses clearly explain the article's organization by naming the structural components (for example, an attention-grabbing introduction, labeled sections like 'What Is Space Junk?', 'Crash Course', and 'Little Bits, But a Big Deal', chronological or problem–solution sequences, or thematic divisions) and support that explanation with specific, text-based details such as headings, examples, or the order in which ideas are presented.

changes.0: Changed teacher-focused wording to response-focused; specified examples of the kinds of inaccurate or unrelated statements seen in responses and noted lack of text-based support.
changes.1: Shifted to response-perspective and added concrete indicators (missing section titles, lack of textual examples) to differentiate these answers from full proficiency.
changes.2: Rephrased from student-focused to response-focused and added explicit references to possible organizational elements and required text-based evidence to distinguish from partial answers.

10.question: What is the effect of different lid colors on the air temperature inside a glass jar exposed to a lamp?

human_rubrics.2: The response uses the results from the experiment to describe the best paint color for the doghouse by choosing a color and: (1) describing how that color might affect the inside of the doghouse, and (2) using results from the experiment to support the description.
human_rubrics.1: The response chooses a color and describes how that color might affect the inside of the doghouse BUT the results from the experiment are missing —OR— the response chooses a color and includes results from the experiment BUT the description of how that color might affect the inside of the doghouse is missing.
human_rubrics.0: The response demonstrates little or no understanding of the Content Standard.

rubrics.0: Responses give little or no accurate explanation about how lid color affects the air temperature inside the jar, often consisting of a single color choice, unrelated statements, or garbled text. They do not connect darker colors to greater heat absorption and provide no usable experimental temperatures or reasoning to support any claim.
rubrics.1: Responses either name a color and state a qualitative effect (for example, ‘white keeps it cooler’ or ‘black gets hotter’) without using the experiment’s temperature data, or they report experimental numbers but fail to explain how those numbers show that darker lids absorb more heat. They show partial understanding but omit either the experimental support or the physical explanation linking color to heat absorption.
rubrics.2: Responses recommend a specific lid (paint) color and explicitly describe how that color affects internal air temperature, citing the experiment’s measured temperatures or comparisons (for example, noting that black lids reached higher average temperatures than white lids). They tie the data to the reason that darker surfaces absorb and retain more heat and use those results to justify the choice.

changes.0: Changed teacher-perspective to response-perspective; specified examples of poor responses (single-word, garbled, unrelated) and emphasized lack of experimental data and failure to state heat-absorption concept.
changes.1: Shifted wording to the perspective of responses and clarified the two distinct partial patterns (color claim without data, or data without explanation), distinguishing this from both no-understanding and full-supported answers.
changes.2: Reworded from response-perspective and made explicit that responses must both cite experimental temperatures/comparisons and connect them to the physical explanation that darker lids absorb more heat to justify the recommendation.
